title: How to Work with Resistance and Avoidance During Psychedelic Journeys
body:
How to Work with Resistance and Avoidance During Psychedelic Journeys

You’ve probably heard someone say that psychedelic journeys are about surrender. But what happens when surrender feels impossible? When your mind pushes back, clutches tight, or outright runs? Resistance and avoidance during psychedelic experiences are far more common than you might think. Maybe you’ve felt it yourself - a sudden urge to change the setting, fix your breathing, or distract yourself with music when things get uncomfortable. Or a creeping sense that you’re “not ready” for what’s coming. Look, this is not a failure. It’s part of the process.

Here’s the thing: resistance is your brain’s way of protecting you. It’s a signal, not a roadblock. Avoidance is a defense mechanism that’s kept you safe before. Does that land? But during a psychedelic journey, these old guards can feel like enemies. They can stop healing in its tracks or send you spiraling into frustration or fear. So, how do you work with this stuff when it shows up?

Recognize Resistance as a Messenger

First, don’t fight it. When you notice resistance, try to name it. What does it feel like in your body? Tight chest? Shaky hands? A racing mind? For example, during one of my early journeys, I felt my throat tightening whenever a difficult memory surfaced. Instead of pushing through recklessly, I paused and said out loud, “I see you, resistance.” That simple act shifted the energy.

Resistance is often fear wearing a mask. Fear of pain, loss, change, or even just the unknown. When you acknowledge it, you start a conversation instead of a battle. It’s like when you’re talking to a scared friend - you don’t yell at them to get over it. You listen, you hold space. You do the same with yourself.

Use Grounding Techniques to Stay Present

It’s easy to get swallowed by avoidance, which can look like zoning out, fiddling with your phone, or distracting yourself with music or movement. Sometimes these are helpful. Sometimes they’re not. The key is to check in: is this grounding me or running me away? If it’s the latter, try these concrete steps to stay present with what’s unfolding.

  • Find your breath: Slow, deep breaths. Count to four on the inhale, hold for two, exhale for six. Repeat. It’s simple but powerful.
  • Feel your body: Press your feet into the floor or the ground beneath you. Wiggle your toes. Notice the texture of the blanket around you - like a soft therapy blanket (paid link) you can wrap around your shoulders.
  • Use an anchor word: Pick a word like “safe” or “here.” Repeat it quietly to yourself whenever the mind starts to drift.

These tools don’t erase resistance. They help you stay present enough to feel it without being swallowed by it.

Invite Curiosity, Not Judgment

Resistance often comes with a heavy dose of self-judgment: “I’m not doing this right,” or “I’m weak.” Throw those thoughts out immediately. They don’t help anyone. Instead, try to meet resistance with curiosity. Ask yourself, “What is this trying to tell me?”

Maybe you feel resistance because a specific memory or emotion is too raw. Maybe the part of you that resists is protecting a vulnerable inner child. I once worked with a client named Sara who kept avoiding a painful childhood memory during her sessions. Instead of pushing her to face it head-on, I invited her to describe what the resistance felt like. She said, “It’s like a wall of ice.” That metaphor opened the door. We didn’t rush in. We just noticed the ice. Over time, Sara’s relationship with that memory softened and her resistance melted.

Curiosity creates space for healing. Judgment shuts it down.

Set Clear Intentions Before You Begin

Resistance can sneak up when your mind doesn’t know what to expect or feels out of control. Before you start your journey, make your intentions clear but flexible. Write them down if you want. Something like, “I want to explore my relationship with anxiety” or “I’m open to whatever comes, even if it’s uncomfortable.”

This isn’t about controlling the experience - far from it. It’s about giving your mind an anchor, a north star to come back to when things feel chaotic. Intentions can be as simple as “I’m here to learn.”

Also, prepare your space. A quiet, comfortable room with minimal distractions helps. Maybe you bring a few items that soothe you, like a gentle meditation timer (paid link) to mark your session or some calming music on standby. Having these ready can reduce the urge to avoid.

Develop Your Support System

You don’t have to do this alone. Resistance and avoidance can feel overwhelming if you’re isolated. A trusted trip sitter or guide who understands the terrain can make a huge difference. They can remind you to breathe, help you come back to the present, and check in with you when you start pulling away.

One friend of mine, James, swore by having a sitter who simply said, “I’m here with you,” when his mind started racing. The simple presence helped him face his inner turbulence instead of running.

If you’re journeying solo, consider journaling immediately before and after your session. Write down what you’re noticing about resistance and avoidance. Sometimes putting it on paper helps make the invisible visible.

Understand The Science Behind Resistance

Resistance during psychedelic experiences isn’t just psychological - it’s biological. Psychedelics temporarily change brain activity, especially in areas related to the default mode network, which controls your sense of self and habitual thinking. When that network quiets, suppressed thoughts and feelings can bubble up. This can feel like chaos, triggering your brain’s natural defense mechanisms.

Studies from the National Institutes of Health show that this brain activity shift can lead to intense emotional responses. Resistance is your brain’s way of trying to keep things "safe" as it recalibrates. Knowing this helps you realize resistance isn’t personal failure, but a neurobiological response. It’s a signal your system is working, even if it feels messy.

Work with Resistance, Don’t Fight It

So, what does working with resistance look like? It’s less about force and more about gentle engagement. Here are some practical steps:

  • Pause and breathe: When you feel resistance, stop what you’re doing. Take a moment to breathe deeply and feel your body.
  • Ask questions: What is this feeling? Where do I feel it? What does it want me to know?
  • Use tools: Consider bringing a precision milligram scale (paid link) if you’re microdosing to measure carefully and avoid surprises that trigger resistance.
  • Write it down: Keep a notebook nearby. Sometimes writing a sentence or two can shift your perspective.
  • Talk to your sitter or guide: Share what you’re feeling. Sometimes just naming resistance aloud breaks its power.

Remember, resistance will often lessen if you don’t try to overpower it. It’s like a wave - you don’t swim against it, you ride it out.

Know When to Take Breaks

Honestly, sometimes resistance means you need a break. If the session becomes too intense or you feel overwhelmed, it’s okay to pause. Drink water. Lie down. Wrap yourself in something comforting, like a soft therapy blanket (paid link). Let your body rest.

Resistance isn’t a sign that you have to push harder. It can be a signal that your mind or body needs time to integrate what’s coming up. Taking breaks doesn’t mean you failed. It means you’re honoring your limits.

Practice Integration After the Journey

Resistance and avoidance don’t vanish once the psychedelic wears off. They often show up during integration - the days and weeks following your session. This is when you take the insights you gained and bring them into daily life. Resistance here might look like procrastinating on journaling, avoiding therapy, or ignoring your feelings.

One simple practice I recommend is setting a daily 5-minute check-in with yourself. Use an intermittent fasting tracker (paid link) or any timer to remind you. Sit quietly, breathe, and ask, "What am I avoiding right now?" No pressure to fix anything, just noticing.

You can also read more on how to integrate psychedelic experiences effectively in my article Psychedelic Integration 101. It covers practical steps that help you stay grounded and move through resistance after the journey.

Final Thoughts

Resistance and avoidance during psychedelic journeys are not problems to be solved. They’re parts of you trying to find safety in unfamiliar territory. When you meet them with kindness, curiosity, and patience, they become guides instead of enemies. You don’t have to rush. You don’t have to fight.

Look, this path isn’t about perfection or pushing past pain at all costs. It’s about learning how to be with yourself in every moment, even the uncomfortable ones. That’s the real medicine.

If you want to learn more about conscious healing and psychedelic wellness, visit my site for resources and support.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
